one_line_takeaway: By confining both an LLM defender and an LLM attacker to finite, tool-enforced action catalogs, the authors derive a Lean-4-machine-checked Lyapunov/ISS stability certificate that is architectural (provably holds for any controller/adversary drawn from the catalogs) and empirically show it holds on 282 real enterprise attack graphs and a paired-telemetry GOAD scenario, while also showing that catalog-bounded stability does not guarantee a strong (or even good) game-theoretic outcome -- Claude Haiku 4.5 stays fully within the safety envelope yet still converges to a suboptimal defense in half its runs.
key_finding: On Experiment 1 (282 real enterprise graphs, deterministic greedy controller): defender-only S(k) is non-increasing on every defender turn in 282/282 graphs (Wilson 95% CI [0.987,1.000]); mean S falls 0.509->0.408 (19.7% reduction, bootstrap 95% CI [17.2%,22.3%], paired Wilcoxon p<1e-24) in 3.0+/-0.1 rounds. Under adversarial expansion, all single-round S spikes stay within the ISS bound (max observed delta-S=0.74 < gamma=1.0; mean max spike 0.42, 95% CI [0.40,0.44]); anticipatory defense blocks 67/890 adversary actions outright (7.5%). Belief-truth gap |S(k)-S-hat(k)| decays geometrically (fit 0.064*0.10^k+0.007) and is counterintuitively 4.7x lower under adversarial pressure than defender-only (median 0.07 vs 0.33, paired Wilcoxon p<1e-32) -- adversary-triggered reveals supply extra Bayesian updates. On Experiment 2 (paired GOAD telemetry): deterministic greedy plateaus at S(k_final)=0.8367 (5 policies deployed); tool-mediated Claude Sonnet 4 reaches S(k_final)=0.3427 (59% lower than greedy) by additionally deploying mfa_enforcement and cloud_app_security, with belief-truth gap 0.0 vs greedy's 0.494. All 40/40 Sonnet 4 runs across four temperatures converge to the identical S(k_final)=0.3427 (zero variance, sigma=0), described as a stronger result than the ~15% accuracy variance at T=0 reported by Atil et al. for LLM non-determinism generally; Kruskal-Wallis across temperatures p=0.17 (no detectable temperature effect on outcome), Defender Jaccard 0.86-0.93 (i.e., action-level variance persists even though outcome does not), zero off-catalog hallucinations across 210 deployments. Claude Haiku 4.5, run through the identical tool-mediated architecture, is catalog-bounded (zero off-catalog hallucinations, ISS-bounded) but has non-zero outcome variance: S(k_final) sigma=0.249, mean 0.603 (95% CI [0.527,0.681]), with 19/40 runs matching Sonnet 4's 0.3427 and 21/40 stalling at 0.85-0.90 (greedy level); traced to a specific reasoning failure (Haiku does not re-query its belief graph after adversary expansion, so 21/40 runs show final belief-truth gap >0.1). Mann-Whitney confirms Sonnet vs Haiku per-run S(k_final) differ, p=1.6e-7. The paper's overall claim is that architectural stability (catalog-bounded, ISS-bounded behavior) is decoupled from achieved game-value optimality, which depends on controller capability.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a tool-mediated architectural pattern for LLM-in-the-loop adversarial control, applied to autonomous cyber defense (EDR policy hardening). An LLM controller (defender) and an LLM adversary (attacker) each select actions only from finite catalogs, via 9 (defender) and 11 (adversary) deterministic tools (e.g. compute_v_after_deploy, simulate_round_ahead, get_critical_path, find_weakest_path, find_zero_day_opportunity); the LLM never computes the game value S(k), enumerates paths, or runs observer updates directly -- it only composes tool outputs and selects catalog actions, with off-catalog proposals rejected as no-ops at the tool-output interface. The authors define a composite Lyapunov function V(k) = S(k) + lambda*theta(k) (game value S(k) via a Stackelberg/network-interdiction formulation, plus mean per-edge Bayesian-observer uncertainty theta(k)) and machine-check, in Lean 4 with Mathlib (five files, ~300 lines, zero sorry), a Theorem 1 establishing (i) controllability (monotone decrease of V absent adversary disturbance), (ii) Input-to-State Stability (ISS) robustness under an adversary that adds a bounded new edge per round, and (iii) observability/convergence of a per-edge Kalman-filter-style Bayesian observer from asymmetric sensor data. Two corollaries extend the certificate to any controller and any adversary drawn from the respective finite catalogs, i.e. the guarantee is architectural (a property of the catalog/tool interface) rather than of the specific LLM's reasoning. This is empirically validated in two experiments (Sec. 5) rather than merely cited from related work.
threat_model: The adversary is an LLM-based attacker agent with full visibility of the true attack graph G(k) (asymmetric relative to the defender) that observes the defender's deployed policies and best-responds (Stackelberg follower), proposing new attack-graph edges from a finite MITRE-derived technique catalog T (66 techniques in Experiment 2) via an 11-tool suite (e.g. find_weakest_path, evaluate_new_edge, find_zero_day_opportunity), constrained to connect existing intermediate nodes (no ENTRY->OBJECTIVE bypass) and to add at most one new edge per round. The defender is an LLM-based EDR-policy controller with only partial/asymmetric visibility (a belief graph built from telemetry with ~40% of edges 'dark'/unobserved in Experiment 1 at 60% synthetic sensor coverage), selecting hardening actions from a finite policy catalog (30 actions spanning endpoint/identity/cloud in Experiment 2) via 9 tools, budget-constrained (B=3 deployments/round). The paper's threat model is explicitly non-destructive/architectural in scope: it does not model prompt injection, jailbreaking, or attacks on the LLM itself, but rather treats the adversary's action-generation process as an 'intelligent disturbance' on a control loop, bounded because both attacker and defender are restricted to finite, pre-vetted action catalogs enforced at the tool-output interface (Assumption A2/A3).
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: Experiment 1 uses synthetic sensor alerts at 60% coverage from a single (unnamed) telemetry-simulation source rather than fully real detection telemetry; Experiment 2 tests only one attack graph (GOAD, 23 nodes/44 edges) and one LLM family (Claude Sonnet 4 / Haiku 4.5), so cross-family and cross-graph generalization of the corollaries is untested. Assumption A4 (no policy rollback/undo) is acknowledged as a modeling restriction whose relaxation is left to future work. The dual-use nature of the result is flagged: Corollary 2 proves the identical certificate for the adversary's action space, meaning the same architectural stability applies to an LLM-driven attacker. The paper also concedes that a certified-stable system (Haiku 4.5) can still make poor decisions, i.e., the Lyapunov/ISS certificate bounds catalog-conformance and boundedness but not decision quality/optimality. 143/282 graphs actually drive non-trivial S reduction (the remaining 139 produce flat trajectories), which the authors report transparently as an effective-N caveat rather than presenting the full 282 as uniformly dynamic.

datasets_used: 282 real enterprise attack graphs derived from Horizon3.ai NodeZero pentest telemetry (stratified sample of 300 from 7,372 operations; 18 excluded; 161 organizations, 25 industries; 276-3,599 edges, median 806)

One Game of Active Directory (GOAD) pentest graph (23 nodes, 44 edges, 30-action defender catalog, 66-technique adversary catalog) with paired Horizon3.ai NodeZero offensive telemetry and Microsoft Defender XDR defensive telemetry (5 hosts, 55-minute pentest)
quantitative_results: Experiment 1 defender-only: S(k) non-increasing in 282/282 graphs (Wilson 95% CI [0.987,1.000]); mean S 0.509 -> 0.408 (19.7% reduction, 95% CI [17.2%,22.3%], p<1e-24) in 3.0+/-0.1 rounds
Experiment 1 ISS: max observed single-round delta-S = 0.74 (bound gamma=1.0); mean max spike 0.42 (95% CI [0.40,0.44]); anticipatory defense blocks 67/890 (7.5%) adversary actions
Experiment 1 observability: belief-truth gap |S(k)-S-hat(k)| median 0.07 (adversarial) vs 0.33 (defender-only), a 4.7x reduction (p<1e-32); geometric fit 0.064*0.10^k+0.007

Experiment 2: deterministic greedy S(k_final)=0.8367; Claude Sonnet 4 tool-mediated S(k_final)=0.3427 (59% lower), zero variance across 40 runs / 4 temperatures; belief-truth gap 0.0 (Sonnet) vs 0.494 (greedy)
Experiment 2: Claude Haiku 4.5 S(k_final) mean 0.603, sigma=0.249 (95% CI [0.527,0.681]); 19/40 runs reach 0.3427, 21/40 stall at 0.85-0.90; Mann-Whitney Sonnet vs Haiku p=1.6e-7
Zero off-catalog hallucinations across 210 deployments (Sonnet 4), 210 deployments (Haiku 4.5), and 420/660 combined actions cited in different aggregations across the paper
Temperature sweep total cost: $93.90 across 80 LLM runs (40 Sonnet 4 + 40 Haiku 4.5)
baselines_compared: Deterministic greedy controller (no LLM, no tool calls; catalog policies ranked purely by marginal S(k) reduction) used both to isolate the architecture's deterministic components in Experiment 1 and as the baseline the LLM controllers are compared against in Experiment 2
